name: Islamic Educational Foundation
mission: ESTABLISH AND OPERATE PRE-SCHOOL, ELEMENTARY, HIGH SCHOOL.
theoryOfChange: The organization states it cultivates civic-minded, professionally successful, God-conscious Muslim leaders through a private Islamic school system that integrates faith and character development with academic excellence (STEM and the Arts), including Islamic Studies and Quranic Studies focused on memorization plus understanding and practical application.
scoreSummary: Islamic Education Foundation of New Jersey Inc shows good impact and limited alignment (minor risk deductions applied).

baseline_narrative.amal_score_rationale: The organization earns a moderate score of 51/100, reflecting strong financial liquidity and efficient fundraising balanced against a need for greater donor alignment and impact transparency.
areas_for_improvement: Enhancing transparency regarding specific program outcomes and third-party impact verification.
Improving alignment with broader donor expectations to increase the overall alignment score.

dimension_explanations.alignment: The organization serves a specific religious and cultural niche within the New Jersey community, though its current donor fit is rated as low [1]. It provides a clear pathway for general charitable giving through sadaqah, but lacks the broader transparency or specialized reporting often sought by diverse Muslim donor segments [3].
dimension_explanations.impact: The foundation demonstrates a high capacity for service delivery, allocating 71.5% of its $5,631,488 total revenue directly to its educational programs [1][2]. While it effectively maintains its infrastructure and capacity building, there is limited publicly available data regarding specific student outcomes or standardized performance metrics [1].

baseline_narrative.headline: The Islamic Education Foundation of New Jersey Inc provides comprehensive faith-based education through its network of elementary and high school programs.
strengths: Maintains a robust working capital of 16.3 months, ensuring long-term operational stability [1].
Demonstrates high fundraising efficiency, spending $0.00 per $1 raised [1].
baseline_narrative.summary: The Islamic Education Foundation of New Jersey Inc is dedicated to establishing and operating educational institutions, including Al-Ghazaly High School and Al-Hikmah Elementary School [1][2]. The organization supports its mission through various giving methods, offering donors the opportunity to contribute via sadaqah to sustain its academic operations [3].

rich_narrative.amal_score_rationale: The organization earns a moderate score of 51/100, reflecting strong financial liquidity and efficient fundraising balanced against a need for greater donor alignment and impact transparency.

areas_for_improvement.context: While academic accreditation is present, there is <cite id="12">no beneficiary data available for cost-per-beneficiary scoring</cite>, making it difficult to assess relative impact efficiency.
The organization currently operates with <cite id="13">minimal board governance consisting of 3 members</cite>, which may limit diverse oversight as the foundation grows.

bbb_assessment.summary: The organization undergoes independent financial audits but is not currently evaluated by the BBB Wise Giving Alliance.

case_against.mitigation_notes: The foundation's 40-year track record and external accreditation mitigate some governance and quality concerns.
risk_factors: <cite id="13">Small board size (3 members)</cite> limits oversight diversity
case_against.summary: The primary concerns involve <cite id="12">limited transparency regarding specific outcome metrics</cite> and a small board size that may pose governance risks.

confidence.notes: Financial data is robust, but impact metrics are limited to instructional days.

alignment.explanation: While the foundation is a <cite id="5">Muslim-focused organization</cite>, its alignment score is moderated because it operates in a <cite id="16">well-covered space with mainstream reach</cite> rather than targeting underserved populations.
alignment.improvement: The foundation could improve alignment by <cite id="17">adding a dedicated zakat page</cite> or clearly stating the zakat-eligibility status of its various programs.

credibility.explanation: Donors can trust the foundation due to its <cite id="1">founding in 1984</cite> and its <cite id="5">Cognia accreditation</cite>, which provides external validation of its operations. The organization also maintains a <cite id="11">71.5% independent board</cite> according to reporting data.
credibility.improvement: To strengthen credibility, the foundation could publish more detailed annual impact reports and <cite id="13">expand its board size</cite> beyond the current three members.

impact.explanation: The foundation shows a <cite id="15">71.5% program expense ratio</cite> in its tax filings, with a delivery model focused on capacity building through education. It provides <cite id="8">183 instructional days</cite> per year to its students.
impact.improvement: Impact measurement would benefit from <cite id="12">publishing consistent beneficiary counts</cite> to allow for cost-effectiveness assessments.

grantmaking_profile.grant_strategy: Direct operation of schools rather than grantmaking

rich_narrative.headline: The Islamic Education Foundation of New Jersey Inc provides comprehensive faith-based education through its network of elementary and high school programs.

ideal_donor_profile.best_for_summary: Best for donors who value long-term investment in Islamic educational infrastructure and want to support established, accredited schools in North America.
donor_motivations: Want to support high-quality Islamic schooling
Value institutional longevity and 40+ years of experience
Prefer organizations with zero fundraising costs
Care about academic accreditation and secular-religious balance
giving_considerations: Donations are classified as sadaqah, not zakat
Focus is on domestic US educational capacity building
Financial reserves are very high, indicating long-term stability rather than immediate cash need
ideal_donor_profile.not_ideal_for: Donors seeking to fulfill zakat obligations or those looking for international emergency relief.

impact_evidence.evidence_grade_explanation: Grade B reflects verified academic accreditation and consistent delivery of instructional days, though specific per-student cost-effectiveness data is missing.
impact_evidence.theory_of_change_summary: The foundation creates impact by providing a structured, accredited K-12 environment that integrates Islamic values with modern academic standards to produce well-rounded community members.
impact_evidence.why_evidence_matters: Accreditation ensures that the educational services provided meet national standards for quality and student success.

long_term_outlook.room_for_funding_explanation: While the foundation has strong reserves, its <cite id="19">12.5% revenue growth</cite> suggests expanding capacity to serve more students.

peer_comparison.differentiator: Distinguished by an <cite id="15">exceptional 71.5% program expense ratio</cite> compared to a <cite id="18">peer median of 89.6%</cite>.

strengths.detail: The organization maintains <cite id="9">16.3 months of working capital</cite>, providing a significant buffer for long-term operational sustainability.
The foundation demonstrates a <cite id="7">fundraising efficiency of $0.00 per $1 raised</cite>, ensuring that donor contributions are not eroded by solicitation costs.
The schools are <cite id="5">accredited by Cognia</cite>, which validates the foundation's commitment to rigorous educational standards.

strengths_deep_dive: The Islamic Education Foundation's primary strength lies in its institutional maturity and financial health. Having operated for <cite id="1">over 40 years</cite>, it has built a substantial asset base, with <cite id="10">total assets reaching $5,926,529</cite> against minimal liabilities. This fiscal discipline is matched by academic rigor; the foundation's schools are cited as being in the <cite id="5">top 20% of private schools in the country</cite>. Furthermore, the organization maintains a <cite id="11">71.5% independent board</cite>, which supports strong oversight and governance for its complex educational operations.
rich_narrative.summary: The Islamic Education Foundation of New Jersey Inc (IEF) is a long-standing educational institution <cite id="1">founded in 1984</cite> to provide comprehensive faith-based schooling in the New Jersey area. The organization operates a network of schools, including <cite id="2">Al-Ghazaly High School and Al-Hikmah Elementary School</cite>, which offer a curriculum spanning preschool through high school. With a <cite id="3">total revenue of $4,637,834</cite> in 2023, the foundation sustains its academic mission primarily through <cite id="4">program service revenue totaling $4,279,911</cite>, reflecting a model largely supported by tuition and educational services. The foundation is recognized for its academic standards, holding <cite id="5">Cognia Accreditation</cite> and being noted as a premier Islamic institution in North America. Beyond core academics, the foundation manages an <cite id="6">Islamic Studies Department and a Quranic Studies & Hifth Program</cite>, ensuring a holistic religious and secular education. Financial data indicates a highly efficient fundraising model with <cite id="7">$0.00 spent to raise every $1</cite>, allowing the organization to focus its resources on its <cite id="8">183-day annual school calendar</cite> and student development.
